coredata.dc:title: Metal free and sunlight driven g-C3N4 based photocatalyst using carbon quantum dots from Arabian dates: Green strategy for photodegradation of 2,4-dichlorophenol and selective detection of Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Sim, Lan Ching
Tai, Jun Yan
Leong, Kah Hon
Saravanan, Pichiah
Tan, Sin Tee
Chong, Woon Chan
Aziz, Azrina Abd
coredata.dc:description: The fabrication of photocatalyst with a visible response and prolonged lifetime of charge carriers is a significant tactic to treat EDCs. In this work, a green synthesis route was adopted to prepare carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from Arabian dates (AD-CQDs) via hydrothermal method. The different weight percentages of fabricated AD-CQDs (10, 15 and 20 wt%) were coupled with graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) to construct AD-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites to degrade 2,4-dicholorophenol (2,4-DCP) under sunlight irradiation with an average light intensity of ~973 × 100 lx. The obtained AD-CQDs were used as a highly selective sensor for ferric (Fe3+) ions, with a low detection limit of 1 nM. The increase loading of AD-CQDs resulted in particle agglomeration which decreased the specific surface area of g-C3N4 from 74.799 m2/g to 62.542 m2/g. The low specific surface area in the composites did not hamper the photocatalytic performance in which all composites showed a higher degradation rate than that of g-C3N4. With the optimum loading of AD-CQDs (20 wt%), the composite degraded 100% of 2,4-DCP in 90 min which was 1.7 times higher than g-C3N4 (59.48%). The excellent photocatalytic performance was mainly correlated to the effective separation of photogenerated electrons as evidenced by TRPL and transient photocurrent response. The second factor is visible light response because of the minor decrease of band gap energy as evidenced in UV–vis DRS spectra. Both factors are attributed to the dual functions of AD-CQDs as electron acceptors and photosensitizers. The simple and low-cost synthesis strategy could be an alternative to obtain sunlight driven photocatalysts without coupling with metal dopants or other semiconductors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preliminary results of photocatalytic degradation of 2,4-DCP for AD-CQDs/g-C3N4 at different weight percentage.

FESEM image of (a) g-C3N4, (b) AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(10), (c) AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(15), (d) AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20), TEM image of (e) AD-CQDs and (f) HRTEM image of AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20). The inset of (b) and (e) shows EDX analysis and particle size distribution of AD-CQDs respectively.
(a) XRD patterns, FTIR spectra of (b) Arabian dates and AD-CQDs, and (c) g-C3N4, AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(10), AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(15), and AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of AD-CQDs solution, (b) Fluorescence emission of AD-CQDs solution at different excitation spectrum, (c) UV–vis DRS and (d) Tauc plots of g-C3N4, AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(10), AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(15), and AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20). Inset of (a) shows fluorescence under daylight (left) and UV light (right).
XPS spectra of (a) C1s, (b) N1s, and (c) O1s of AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20).
(a) TRPL decay spectra and (b) Transient photocurrent response for (i) g-C3N4, (ii) AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(10), (iii) AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(15) and (iv) AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20).

(a) Fluorescence intensity of AD-CQDs towards the detection of different metal ions, (b) PL emission of AD-CQDs solution with different Fe3+concentration and (c) Linear plot of PL intensity against the concentration of Fe3+.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of 2,4-DCP by (a) AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(10), AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(15), AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20), g-C3N4, and blank under sunlight irradiation, (b) Scavenging test using AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20) and (c) Schematic diagram of energy band structure parameters and proposed electron transfer for degradation mechanism of 2,4-DCP.
BET surface area, pore volume and average pore size of the prepared samples.
Correlation between the characteristics and photodegradation efficiency of 2,4-DCP.
A comparative study of the photodegradation of 2,4-DCP.

head.ce:title: Metal free and sunlight driven g-C3N4 based photocatalyst using carbon quantum dots from Arabian dates: Green strategy for photodegradation of 2,4-dichlorophenol and selective detection of Fe3+

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of photocatalyst with a visible response and prolonged lifetime of charge carriers is a significant tactic to treat EDCs. In this work, a green synthesis route was adopted to prepare carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from Arabian dates (AD-CQDs) via hydrothermal method. The different weight percentages of fabricated AD-CQDs (10, 15 and 20 wt%) were coupled with graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) to construct AD-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites to degrade 2,4-dicholorophenol (2,4-DCP) under sunlight irradiation with an average light intensity of ~973 × 100 lx. The obtained AD-CQDs were used as a highly selective sensor for ferric (Fe3+) ions, with a low detection limit of 1 nM. The increase loading of AD-CQDs resulted in particle agglomeration which decreased the specific surface area of g-C3N4 from 74.799 m2/g to 62.542 m2/g. The low specific surface area in the composites did not hamper the photocatalytic performance in which all composites showed a higher degradation rate than that of g-C3N4. With the optimum loading of AD-CQDs (20 wt%), the composite degraded 100% of 2,4-DCP in 90 min which was 1.7 times higher than g-C3N4 (59.48%). The excellent photocatalytic performance was mainly correlated to the effective separation of photogenerated electrons as evidenced by TRPL and transient photocurrent response. The second factor is visible light response because of the minor decrease of band gap energy as evidenced in UV–vis DRS spectra. Both factors are attributed to the dual functions of AD-CQDs as electron acceptors and photosensitizers. The simple and low-cost synthesis strategy could be an alternative to obtain sunlight driven photocatalysts without coupling with metal dopants or other semiconductors.

ce:section.ce:para: The accumulation of Fe3+ originated from pipeline corrosion and industrial wastes effluent including engine parts, metal finishing, battery manufacturing and galvanized pipe manufacturing [1]. The presence of a high amount of Fe3+ ions in water bodies will lead to disease development such as iron-loading, cardiomyopathy, arthropathy, neoplasia, and endocrine disorders [2]. According to the standard parameter for water in Malaysia, Fe3+ ions content must be controlled to the recommended limits which are 5 mg/L in response to the human body health and ecosystem sustainability [3]. The conventional analytical method such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) and etc. are not suitable for in-place analysis due to the heavy instruments required and the complexity of the operation [4]. Therefore, the development of sensitive, selective and inexpensive sensing systems for Fe3+ detection is necessary for environmental concerns. In order to detect heavy metal, techniques based on fluorescence detection are introduced as they are highly sensitivity, inexpensive, easy and able to miniaturize for the ease of in-field applications [5]. The CQDs are more effective fluorophores due to their higher chemical inertness, solubility, resistance towards photobleaching with facile modification of their size and morphology [6].

The luminescence that is originated from the defect or surface state and subdomain state within the graphitic carbon core enables CQDs to function as an optical sensor [7]. Gao et al. (2019) found that lignin derived CQDs were able to detect Fe3+ because of the formation of non-fluorescence complex between CQDs and Fe3+. This CQDs probe exhibited a wide linear range of 50–650 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 196 nM [8]. It was reported that the CQDs were able to detect Hg2+ and Ag+ because the functional groups of CQDs were replaced by metal ions to produce complexes of stable chelate that led to a strong quenching of CQDs luminescence [9,10]. Recently, CQDs were used to activate metal organic frameworks (MOFs) to detect Fe3+ and Cu2+ with LOD 2.3 ppb and 1.3 ppb, respectively. The CQDs detached from MOFs when the coordination of the metal ions with the organic ligands in MOFs was stronger than the hydrogen bonding between MOFs and CQDs. This resulted in the quenching of activated fluorescence [11].

Besides that, the untreated industrial effluents also consist of endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDCs) which was described by World Health Organization (WHO) as an exogenous mixture which altered endocrine system function and subsequently cause harmful effects on human beings [12]. Thus, both contamination of Fe3+ and EDCs require extensive research on both metal sensing and water remediation to develop a better treatment plant for untreated industrial effluents. CQDs based composite system demonstrated excellent potential in the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. The decoration of TiO2 with CQDs could aid in the separation of photogenerated e−-h+ pairs because of the π-π stacking interaction and heterojunction formation between CQDs and TiO2. The removal of phenol using CQDs/TiO2 was 99% [13]. The presence of carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) in respective BiVO4 [14], Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 [15] and W18O49 [16] could promote the efficient light absorption and separation of charge carriers and thus improving the photodegradation of RhB dye. Graphitic carbon nitride is more favorable for visible light absorption owing to its narrower band gap energy (~2.7 eV). The g-C3N4 has gained tremendous attention due to its chemical stability, unique electronic band structure, visible light responsive and simple fabrication method [17,18]. However, g-C3N4 encounters limitations such as lower specific surface area and fast recombination rate of photo-induced charge carriers [19]. Nevertheless, these limitations can be overcome by doping with metal or non-metal impurities, surface coupling hybridization and modification by carbonaceous components such as graphene oxide and carbon dots. Hu et al. (2020) found that the composite of TiO2 nanorod and g-C3N4 was able to degrade 93.4% of ciprofloxacin in 60 min under simulated sunlight irradiation [20]. Huang et al. (2020) loaded zinc stannate (ZnSnO3) onto g-C3N4 nanosheets to form heterojunction photocatalyst which can increase the degradation rate of tetracycline up to 85% in 120 min [21]. Guo et al. (2019) prepared Cl-doped porous g-C3N4 and the composites degraded 92% of tetracycline in 120 min which was 2.4 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4. The Cl-doping can narrow the band gap, increase the specific surface area and enhance the electron mobility of g-C3N4 [22] .

In this work, 2,4-DCP was adopted as the model pollutant because of its lethal health effects. Among the EDCs, 2,4-DCP is the priority pollutant in varied anthropogenic effluents due to its widespread application in insecticide, pesticides, disinfectants and antiseptic fabrication [23]. The recalcitrant property of 2,4-DCP makes it difficult to be removed from clean water bodies as they are stable in molecular structure. The Arabian dates (phoenix dactylifera) were selected as the precursor of CQDs as they contained high lipid content and abundant functional groups for higher yield CQDs conversion [24]. The composition of Arabian dates was reported to be non-reactive and contained various organic and inorganic minerals for safe utilization upon CQDs conversion [25]. As Arabian dates were rarely reported in metal sensing and sunlight driven photocatalytic system, the significance of this work emphasized on its precursor source and application work as a research gap. Herein, the Arabian dates derived CQDs (AD-CQDs) were synthesized via hydrothermal carbonization without the use of harmful chemicals and the resulted AD-CQDs was applied for metal sensing. AD-CQDs were combined with g-C3N4 to form carbon-based composite namely AD-CQDs/g-C3N4 for the photocatalytic degradation of 2,4-DCP under natural sunlight irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AD-CQDs and AD-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites
ce:section.ce:para: Arabian dates (phoenix dactylifera) were purchased from Tesco hypermarket in Kampar, Perak, Malaysia. The hydrothermal carbonization of the mixture of Arabian dates (1 g) and ultrapure water (15 mL) was carried out using Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 200 °C for 3 h [26]. After centrifugation and vacuum drying at 60 °C, the CQDs namely AD-CQDs were obtained. Pure g-C3N4 was prepared by heating urea (99.8%, R&M Chemicals) in the furnace at 550 °C for 2 h. As-obtained g-C3N4 (1 g) was then mixed with different weight percentage of AD-CQDs (10, 15 and 20 wt%). The mixture was poured into autoclave and heated at 100 °C for 2 h, followed by the centrifugation and drying at 60 °C. The obtained composites namely AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(10), AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(15) and AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20) are shown in Fig. S1.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was obtained in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using Perkin Elmer Spectrum 400 spectrophotometer. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted on Panalytical Empyrean diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) from 10° to 80° range at 2° min−1 resolution. The morphological structure and elemental composition were observed using field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Auriga, Zeiss) attached with electron dispersive X-ray study (EDX). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were conducted at 200 kV on FEI-TECNAI F20 Transmission Electron Microscope. Ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were recorded on Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as reference in the range 200–800 nm. The chemical state information was studied by using PHI Quantera II X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with AlKα radiation at 1487 eV. The specific surface area was measured by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method using nitrogen adsorption equipment (Micromeritics ASAP 2020). The absorbance and photoluminescence (PL) of AD-CQDs solution were analyzed on Hach DR6000 UV–vis spectrophotometer and Perkin Elmer LS 55 PL spectrophotometer (λex = 325 nm), respectively. The lifetime of charge carriers was determined by Time Resolved Photoluminescence (TRPL, Edinburgh FLS 920) with excitation wavelength of 480 nm. Transient photocurrent response was recorded at 10 s intervals on Ivium Tech Vertex potentiostat under irradiation of solar simulator (Newport AM 1.5G).

0.1 g/L of AD-CQDs solution and 1 mM concentration of metal ions stock solutions (Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Sn2+ and Sr2+ ions) were prepared for metal sensing. The precursors for metal ion are listed in Table S1. To evaluate the sensitivity of AD-CQDs towards Fe3+, the fluorescence emission analysis was done by adjusting the Fe3+ ions concentration to 0, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 nM.

The photodegradation of 2,4-DCP was conducted under sunlight irradiation at Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman, Perak at the longitude 4.3394° N and latitude 101.1398° E between 12.00 pm and 2.00 pm in March 2021. The sunlight intensity was recorded by using light meter HS1010A of 2000 × 100 lx. The sunlight intensity was recorded in Table S2. The mixture of AD-CQDs/g-C3N4 (0.05 g) and 2,4-DCP solution (10 mg/L) was left in the dark for 2 h prior to the photocatalysis. The 2,4-DCP concentration was analyzed at 15 min interval using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent) equipped with Ascentis C18 column (Supelco Analytical, USA) and fluorescence detector. Acetonitrile (ACN) and water was used as a mobile phase in the ratio of 80:20 flowing at 0.8 mL/min [26]. An UV detector was used to quantify 2,4-DCP with an analytical wavelength of 280 nm at 2.8 min retention time. A scavenger test was conducted by using AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20) to determine the most reactive radicals. 2 mM of isopropyl alcohol (IPA), dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na+) and benzoquinone (BQ) were used to trap OH, e−, h+, and O2 −respectively. The sunlight intensity of the scavenging test was shown in Table S3.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the degradation efficiency of AD-CQDs/g-C3N4 was obviously improved after increasing AD-CQDs to 10 wt%. This preliminary study predicts that AD-CQDs could be increased beyond 10 wt% to improve the degradation efficiency. Herein, 10, 15 and 20 wt% was adopted to optimize the performance of the composites.

The lamellar and wrinkled structure of g-C3N4 nanosheets was clearly observed in Fig. 2(a). Fig. 2(b)–(d) shows that AD-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites agglomerated upon the addition of CQDs. At higher loading, the clustering effect was significant which reduced the specific surface area of g-C3N4 from 74.799 m2/g to 62.542 m2/g (Table 1 ). This also resulted in the reduction of pore volume from 0.474 cm3/g to 0.452 cm3/g. Higher specific surface area and higher pore volume were favorable in photocatalysis because they allowed a rapid diffusion of pollutants towards the photocatalyst [27]. The inset of Fig. 2(b) shows the EDX spectra of C, N and O element that was present in the AD-CQDs/g-C3N4 composite. The O element was formed during the pyrolysis of urea that reacted with oxygen. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2(e), the particle size of AD-CQDs was found in the range from 1.72 nm to 5.85 nm with an average particle size of 3.79 nm. The HRTEM image in Fig. 2(f) shows that AD-CQDs possessed graphitic structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (1 0 0) plane lattice of graphite [28–30].

As shown in Fig. 3(a), all samples exhibited two characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 at 12.8° and 27.6°, corresponding to (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) planes (JCPDS 87-1526) [31,32]. The small peak at 12.8° originated from the in-plane structural packing of tri-s-triazine units [33] while the apparent peak at 27.6° reflected the interplanar stacking of aromatic rings [34]. At higher loading of AD-CQDs, the diffraction angle shifted by 0.3°, indicating the alteration of lattice structure after the incorporation of AD-CQDs. The intensity of (1 0 0) and (0 0 2) peak decreased with the increasing weight percentage of AD-CQDs, suggesting a reduction in the number of stacking layers of g-C3N4 [35]. The coupling with AD-CQDs did not alter the crystalline phase of host structure in all AD-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites due to the low loading amount of AD-CQDs [36–37].

The FTIR of Arabian dates and AD-CQDs was shown in Fig. 3(b). The respective broad peak at 3302 cm−1 (Arabian dates) and 3401 cm−1 (AD-CQDs) was accredited to the absorption band of OH group [38]. The absorption band at 2922 cm−1 (Arabian dates) and 2933 cm−1 (AD-CQDs) was attributed to CH group [39]. Arabian dates contained CO and COO− as seen in 1635 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1, respectively. The significant peak at 1028 cm−1 was represented as COH which corresponded to the main oxygen groups in plant cellulose of Arabian dates [40]. The absorption band at 2933 cm−1 (AD-CQDs) was assigned to the bending vibrations of CH groups [39]. The significant peaks at 1617 cm−1, 1333 cm−1, 1052 cm−1 and 776 cm−1 in AD-CQDs were ascribed to CO, CC, (CH or CO) and CH bending, respectively [41–43]. In all AD-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites, the broad band appeared at 3401 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching of OH (Fig. 3(c)). The band located at 3222 cm−1 and 3013 cm−1 was attributed to amide NH stretching [44]. The peak at 1240 cm−1 represented the CN stretching vibration of amine [45]. The stretching band at 1440–1400 cm−1 was ascribed to amine III CN bond [46]. The sharp absorption band at 814 cm−1 represented CH bending [41].

Fig. 4(a) shows that AD-CQDs can harvest photons from ultraviolet to visible regions. The absorption peak that appeared in the range of 301–371 nm was due to the n-π* transition of CO bonds [47]. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4(a), AD-CQDs solution was brown in colour under daylight and emitted blue-green PL under UV excitation at 365 nm. Fig. 4(b) shows the optical properties of AD-CQDs solution under excitation from 320 to 540 nm. The PL emission of AD-CQDs was dependent on the excitation wavelength. The red-shifted emission from 430 nm to 525 nm was consistent with the observation under UV light (Fig. 4(a)). An optimum emission was observed at blue band, whereas the emission became weaker at green band. The excitation dependent behavior was due to the existence of abundant surface groups that varied the energy levels and transitions [48,49]. According to Sun et al. (2018), the widely distributed transitions dominated the emission under different excitations. Their group revealed that oxidized groups containing CO were responsible for the green emission, whereas the blue luminescence was related to hydroxyl groups at the edge or in the plane [49]. This is consistent with the observation of CO and OH groups in AD-CQDs which resulted in the blue-green PL emission (Fig. 3(b)).

As shown in Fig. 4(c), all AD-CQDs/g-C3N4 composites showed a minor red shifted absorption. The increasing loading of AD-CQDs narrowed the band gap of g-C3N4 from 2.97 eV to 2.88 eV. The band gap of all samples was calculated using Tauc plot with Kubelka-Munck function F(R) as shown in Fig. 4(d). The π conjugated AD-CQDs could act as a photosensitizer to inject more photogenerated electrons to the conduction band (CB) of g-C3N4. The photosensitizing ability could be due to the abundant oxygen-containing groups in AD-CQDs [50] which is consistent with the FTIR results in Fig. 3(b). Although the addition of AD-CQDs enhanced the NIR absorption, the higher wavelength NIR could not be converted to shorter wavelength UV or visible light for the ease of electrons' excitation. This is due to the absence of upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) properties in AD-CQDs (Fig. 4(b)). Nevertheless, the origin of UCPL is questionable. Contrary to the frequently cited UCPL phenomenon [51,52], it was demonstrated that the UCPL could originate from the normal fluorescence excited by the leaking component from the second diffraction in the monochromator of the fluorescence spectrophotometer [53].

Fig. 5 shows the composition and elemental chemical states of AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20). Fig. 5(a) shows the deconvolution of C1s spectrum into four peaks attributed to CC (284.55 eV), CO/CO (285.75 eV), CN (287.75 eV) and COOH (288.19 eV). The sp2-hybridized CC and CN bonds represent the graphitic structure of the g-C3N4 [41]. The carbon atoms were functionalized with oxygen, resulting in the presence of carbonyl (CO) and carboxyl group (COOH). For N1s spectrums in Fig. 5(b), the main peak at 398.34 eV (CNC) was attributed to sp2-bonded N in triazine rings [54]. The two weak peaks at 399.58 eV and 400.93 eV corresponded to the tertiary N atoms N-(C)3 and amino functional groups (CNH), respectively [55]. For O1s spectrum in Fig. 5(c), the peak at 531.30 eV and 532.75 eV was ascribed to the physically adsorbed oxygen on the surface of AD-CQDs and isolated OH/CO/OCO functional groups, respectively [41,56].

TRPL analysis was conducted to examine the lifetime of the photogenerated charge carriers in the samples. Table S4 lists the fitted parameters of TRPL analysis. Fig. 6(a) shows that the TRPL spectra decayed exponentially. The charge carrier lifetime decreased in the sequence of AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20) (4.117 ns) > AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(15) (3.962 ns) > AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(10) (2.272 ns) > g-C3N4 (1.865 ns). Photoelectrochemical measurement was performed to further verify the TRPL results. Fig. 6(b) shows the photocurrent responses to on-off cycles in all samples. The AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20) exhibited the highest photocurrent response in comparison with g-C3N4 and other composites with lower loading of AD-CQDs. Obviously, the higher loading of AD-CQDs could suppress the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers and prolong the lifetime of e−-h+ pairs through the formation of type-II van der Waals heterojunction [57]. The band alignment in the special heterojunction interface can promote the separation of e−-h+ pairs [58], leading to remarkable photocatalytic degradation efficiency of 2,4-DCP.

The AD-CQDs were applied to detect various metal ions such as Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Sn2+ and Sr2+ ions. The metal sensing was conducted to determine the specific selectivity and sensitivity of AD-CQDs towards heavy metal ions. AD-CQDs are suitable to be used as a metal ion probe due to their tunable luminescence ability with excellent photostability, solubility and biocompatibility properties [59]. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the fluorescence emission of AD-CQDs was significantly quenched by Fe3+ while the other metal ions exerted insignificant effects. As shown in FTIR results, there are hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of AD-CQDs which could act as chelating ligands to form a stable complex with Fe3+ [60–62]. This resulted in the fluorescence quenching due to the high selectivity of AD-CQDs towards Fe3+. In addition, Fe3+ displayed a strong absorption peak in the range of 220–350 nm (Fig. S2) which overlapped with the absorption peak of AD-CQDs in the range of 301–371 nm (Fig. 4(a)). The light absorption competition occurred between Fe3+ and AD-CQDs decreased the light absorption of AD-CQDs which reduced the excitation of electrons from VB to CB. Consequently, this led to the fluorescence quenching of AD-CQDs [63,64]. The PL emission of AD-CQDs was recorded by varying the concentration of Fe3+ at 0, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 nM to study the detection sensitivity. Fig. 7(b) shows a remarkable decrease in PL intensity upon the addition of Fe3+ with increasing concentration from 0 nM to 1000 nM. The AD-CQDs displayed a good linear relationship from 0 to 1000 nM with a R2 of 0.99215 (Fig. 7(c)). The linear equation was fitted as (Fo − F) / Fo = 0.56306Ci where Fo and F refer to the fluorescence intensity of AD-CQDs solution with absence and the presence of Fe3+ ions. Ci refers to the concentration of Fe3+ ion. This reveals that AD-CQDs are sensitive to detect Fe3+ ions as low as 1 nM concentration.

As shown in Fig. 8(a), the photolysis of 2,4-DCP shows that there is almost no degradation in the absence of photocatalyst under sunlight irradiation. All composites exhibited better photocatalytic performance than that of g-C3N4. The AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20) achieved the highest degradation rate of 2,4-DCP at 100% in 90 min, followed by AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(15) (93.19%), AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(10) (88.69%) and g-C3N4 (59.48%). The experimental data were fitted into pseudo-first order kinetic model to find the rate constant (k) as tabulated in Table S5. The rate constant of AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20) is 3.15 times higher than that of g-C3N4. This indicates that higher loading of AD-CQDs could boost the degradation performance of g-C3N4 by enhancing the charge separation which is consistent with TRPL and photoelectrochemical measurement. It was reported that the main factors that contributed to the enhancement of photocatalytic degradation efficiency were specific surface area, visible light absorption and charge carrier lifetime [65]. Table 2 shows the correlation between these contributing factors and the photodegradation efficiency for each sample. Although the specific surface area of all composites decreased after coupling with AD-CQDs, the performance of all composites remained unaffected and showed a higher degradation rate than g-C3N4. In this case, the specific surface area was not a contributing factor in which the lifetime of charge carriers was a primary factor that imposed significant effect on the photodegradation efficiency. The secondary factor is the visible light response due to the slight reduction of band gap energy as indicated in UV–vis DRS spectra.

The principal reactive radical species involved in the degradation of 2,4-DCP was investigated using a radical scavenging detection (Fig. 8(b)). It was conducted using AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20) which showed excellent photocatalytic performance among the other composites. Four different scavengers such as BQ, EDTA-2Na+, DMSO and IPA were added into the targeted pollutant to serve as a scavenger for superoxide anions radical (O2 −), holes (h+), electron (e−) and hydroxyl radical (OH), respectively. A remarkable suppression in the photocatalytic efficiency was observed upon adding BQ indicator, indicating that O2 − was the main reactive species involved in the degradation process. The contribution of other reactive species such as h+, OH and e− was insignificant at a similar inhibition level. The edge positions of CB and valence band (VB) in a semiconductor was estimated considering the Mulliken electronegativity rules [66] through the following equations:
(1)
EVB = X − EC + 0.5 Eg
(2)
ECB = EVB − Eg
where E g is band gap energy of the semiconductor, E c is energy of free electrons in the hydrogen scale (4.5 eV), X is Mulliken electronegativity, E VB and E CB is respective VB and CB edge potential. The X value of g-C3N4 was reported to be 4.72 eV [67]. The obtained E g of g-C3N4 is 2.97 eV.

The energy band structure parameters of g-C3N4, AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(10), AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(15), and AD-CQDs/g-C3N4(20) are illustrated in Fig. 8(c). The degradation mechanism and the behavior of electron transfer are proposed in Fig. 8(c). Under sunlight irradiation, the π conjugated AD-CQDs were responsive to a large portion of visible light in the solar spectrum and could act as a photosensitizer like organic dyes to sensitize g-C3N4 and donated the electrons to the CB of g-C3N4. Meantime, g-C3N4 could absorb visible light, exciting the e− from the VB to the CB. The electrons are then injected to AD-CQDs due to the low work functions of the AD-CQDs [68]. The energy band structure is favorable for the generation of O2 − radicals because the calculated CB edge potential of all samples is more negative than the standard redox potential of O2/O2 − (−0.33 eV vs. NHE) [69]. This indicates that the O2 molecule dissolved in 2,4-DCP could be reduced by the photoexcited electrons in g-C3N4 and AD-CQDs to form O2 − radical. The O2 − radicals further reacted with H2O to form OH radical. The VB edge potential of all samples is less positive than the standard redox potential of H2O/OH (+2.38 eV vs. NHE) and OH−/OH (+1.99 eV vs. NHE) [70], thus H2O or hydroxide (OH−) could not be oxidized to form OH radical. This observation is consistent with the scavenging results that propose O2 − to be the key radical species responsible for photodegradation of 2,4-DCP. The formation of OH radical might be represented by Eqs. (3) and (4) [71]:
(3)
˙
O 2
−
+
H 2
O → ˙ OOH +
OH −
(4)
H 2
O + ˙ OOH +
e −
→ ˙ OH +
OH −

Wu et al. (2020) also suggested that O2 − played a significant role in the photodegradation experiment using carbon dots/g-C3N4 hollow porous nanospheres and the presence of O2 − and OH was further confirmed with the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) [68]. Both of OH and O2 − radicals are highly reactive and could oxidize the 2,4-DCP to CO2 and water. On the other hand, AD-CQDs can act as an electron acceptor to trap the electrons injected from the CB of g-C3N4 because of the inherent sp2 graphitic carbon compositions [72]. This explains the increase in charge carriers' lifetime after coupling with AD-CQDs which is in line with the TRPL and transient photocurrent response. Table 3 shows the comparison between other photocatalysts and the prepared sample in this study. This reveals that our work could achieve a better performance than that of other photocatalysts at lesser catalyst dosage and shorter reaction time. Furthermore, the simple preparation method and low cost of bioprecursor provided a practical solution for the removal of 2,4-DCP using natural sunlight.

In summary, Arabian dates with high lipid content and rich functional groups are suitable to be used as a precursor to produce AD-CQDs via hydrothermal carbonization. As prepared AD-CQDs could be used for metal sensing and coupled with g-C3N4 for the degradation of 2,4-DCP. The average particle size of AD-CQDs was found to be 3.79 nm. The loading of AD-CQDs slightly altered the lattice structure by shifting the diffraction peak assigned for interplanar stacking of aromatic rings of g-C3N4 to a higher angle. The hydroxyl and carboxyl group in AD-CQDs was responsible for fluorescence quenching and blue-green emission. The energy band structure of all composites was more favorable for the formation of O2 − radical as evidenced in the radical scavenging test. The optimum loading of AD-CQDs was found to be 20 wt% which removed 100% of 2,4-DCP in 90 min under sunlight irradiation. The enhancement of the photocatalytic activity is mainly due to the following three reasons: (i) formation of type-II van der Waals heterojunction that facilitated the interfacial charge transfer between AD-CQDs and g-C3N4; (ii) AD-CQDs act as an electron trapper to capture the electron injected from the CB of g-C3N4, inhibiting the recombination of charge carriers; (iii) AD-CQDs act as a photosensitizer to extend the visible light absorption in g-C3N4 and donate electrons to CB of g-C3N4. Our work incorporates the element of sustainability in photocatalysis by utilizing natural sunlight as an irradiation source and carbon based photocatalyst derived from green precursor for practical applications in environmental remediation.
